---
title: Fifth Tutorial for R4DS
author: David Kane
tutorial:
  id: r4ds-5
output:
  learnr::tutorial:
    df_print: default
    progressive: true
    allow_skip: true
runtime: shiny_prerendered
description: 'Working with hierarchical JSON data, web scraping with rvest and httr2, and custom functions, using earthquake and IMDb movie datasets.'
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
library(learnr)
library(tutorial.helpers)

library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(rvest)
library(ggrepel)
library(httr2)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(out.width = '90%')
options(tutorial.exercise.timelimit = 60,
        tutorial.storage = "local")

convert_earthquake_time <- function(milliseconds) {
  as.POSIXct(milliseconds / 1000, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC")
}
categorize_magnitude <- function(mag) {
  case_when(
    mag < 3.0 ~ "Minor",
    mag < 4.0 ~ "Light",
    mag < 5.0 ~ "Moderate",
    mag < 6.0 ~ "Strong",
    mag < 7.0 ~ "Major",
    TRUE ~ "Great"
  )
}

earthquake_df <- jsonlite::read_json("data/earthquakes.geojson") |>
  pluck("features") |>
  tibble(data = _) |>
  unnest_wider(data) |>
  unnest_wider(properties, names_sep = "_") |>
  hoist(geometry, coordinates = "coordinates") |>
  unnest_wider(coordinates, names_sep = "_") |>
  rename(
    longitude = coordinates_1,
    latitude  = coordinates_2,
    depth     = coordinates_3
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~ifelse(.x == "null", NA, .x))) |>
  filter(!if_any(c(properties_mag, longitude, latitude), is.na)) |>
  mutate(
    datetime = convert_earthquake_time(as.numeric(properties_time)),
    mag_category = categorize_magnitude(as.numeric(properties_mag))
  ) |>
  mutate(across(c(properties_mag, depth), as.numeric)) |>
  filter(datetime >= Sys.Date() - 365) |>
  filter(longitude >= -125, longitude <= -66, latitude >= 20, latitude <= 50) |>
  mutate(
    hour = hour(datetime),
    day_of_week = wday(datetime, label = TRUE),
    depth_category = case_when(
      depth < 70 ~ "Shallow",
      depth < 300 ~ "Intermediate",
      depth < 600 ~ "Deep"
    )
  ) |>
  select(longitude, latitude, properties_mag, properties_place, datetime, depth, mag_category, depth_category) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "Magnitude: ", properties_mag, " (", mag_category, ")<br>",
      "Location: ", properties_place, "<br>",
      "Time: ", format(datetime, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"), "<br>",
      "Depth: ", round(depth, 1), " km (", depth_category, ")"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(
    longitude >= -180, longitude <= 180,
    latitude >= -90, latitude <= 90,
    properties_mag >= 0, properties_mag <= 10
  ) |>
  mutate(
    color = case_when(
      properties_mag >= 6 ~ "red",
      properties_mag >= 5 ~ "orange",
      properties_mag >= 4 ~ "yellow",
      properties_mag >= 3 ~ "green",
      TRUE ~ "blue"
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(properties_mag))

top_movies <- request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
  req_perform() |>
  resp_body_html() |>
  html_element("table") |>
  html_table() |>
  select(
    rank_title_year = `Rank & Title`,
    rating = `IMDb Rating`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    rank_title_year = str_replace_all(rank_title_year, "\n +", " "),
    rating_details = request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
      req_perform() |>
      resp_body_html() |>
      html_elements("td strong") |>
      html_attr("title")
  ) |>
  separate_wider_regex(
    rank_title_year,
    patterns = c(
      rank = "\\d+", "\\. ",
      title = ".+", " +\\(",
      year = "\\d+", "\\)"
    )
  ) |>
  separate_wider_regex(
    rating_details,
    patterns = c(
      "[0-9.]+ based on ",
      number = "[0-9,]+",
      " user ratings"
    )
  ) |>
  mutate(
    number = parse_number(number),
    across(c(rank, year), as.numeric),
    decade = paste0(floor(year/10)*10, "s"),
    rating_category = case_when(
      rating >= 9.0 ~ "Masterpiece (9.0+)",
      rating >= 8.5 ~ "Excellent (8.5-8.9)",
      rating >= 8.0 ~ "Great (8.0-8.4)",
      TRUE ~ "Good (<8.0)"
    ),
    popularity = case_when(
      number >= 2000000 ~ "Very Popular (2M+)",
      number >= 1000000 ~ "Popular (1M-2M)",
      number >= 500000 ~ "Moderate (500K-1M)",
      TRUE ~ "Niche (<500K)"
    )
  ) |>
  select(rank, title, year, rating, number) |>
  arrange(rank)
```

```{r info-section, child = system.file("child_documents/info_section.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```

## Introduction
###

This section covers key concepts from [Chapter 23: Hierarchical Data](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/hierarchical), [Chapter 24: Web Scraping](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/webscraping), [Chapter 25: Functions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/functions), and [Chapter 26: Iterations](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/iteration) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You will learn about working with nested data structures, web scraping techniques, and creating custom functions using core packages such as **[jsonlite](https://cran.r-project.org/package=jsonlite)**, **[rvest](https://rvest.tidyverse.org/)**, **[httr2](https://httr2.r-lib.org/)**, and **[purrr](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/)**. Key concepts include extracting nested elements from lists, expanding list-columns into separate columns, extracting tables from HTML pages, and splitting text into structured columns using regex patterns.

We recommend using an in-environment AI agent such as [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) or [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code); instructions are written with these tools in mind. You may also use a chat-based AI, but you will need to copy/paste code and data context manually.

### Exercise 1

Create a new repo using `codespace-starter` as a template and name it `r4ds-5`.

Open the `r4ds-5` repo on GitHub and create a Codespace from it.

Create a new file and save it as `analysis.qmd`. Add a YAML header at the top with a title (`"Earthquakes and Movies"`) and your name as author. In a bash terminal, run:

```
quarto render analysis.qmd
```

Open `analysis.html` in a browser by right-clicking it in the File Explorer and selecting **Open with Live Server**. Keep this tab open — it will refresh automatically whenever you re-render.

Create a `.gitignore` file with `analysis_files` on the first line and then a blank line. Save and push.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file(".gitignore")
```

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

List-columns tend to come in two basic forms: named and unnamed. **tidyr** provides `unnest_wider()` for named list-columns — where each row has the same element names — and `unnest_longer()` for unnamed ones where the number of elements varies per row.

### Exercise 2

In your QMD, paste the following `library()` commands in a new code chunk with `#| message: false`:

```
library(tidyverse)
library(leaflet)
library(rvest)
library(httr2)
library(ggrepel)
```

Also add the following to the YAML header to remove all code from the HTML output:

```
execute:
  echo: false
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

JSON is a machine-readable format with six data types: null (like R's `NA`), string, number, boolean (lowercase `true`/`false`), array (like unnamed R lists), and object (like named R lists).

### Exercise 3

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab — it should refresh automatically.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 8)
```

###

`#| cache: true` is a render-time feature — Quarto runs the code on the first render and saves results to disk. Subsequent renders load from the cache instead of re-running. Add `analysis_cache` to `.gitignore` so cache files stay off GitHub.

### Exercise 4

In the R Terminal, run:

```
dir.create("data")
list.files()
```

This creates a `data` directory in your project for storing downloaded files.

CP/CR.

```{r introduction-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

**jsonlite** also provides `fromJSON()`, which performs automatic simplification (`simplifyVector = TRUE`). This often works well for simple JSON, but `read_json()` gives you more control over rectangling complex nested structures.

## Earthquakes
###

This section focuses on working with hierarchical JSON data structures and creating custom functions using **[jsonlite](https://cran.r-project.org/package=jsonlite)**, **[tidyr](https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/)**, and **[leaflet](https://rstudio.github.io/leaflet/)**. You will learn how to extract nested data from GeoJSON files, convert Unix timestamps to datetime objects, and create interactive maps. Key concepts include `pluck()`, `unnest_wider()`, `hoist()`, and writing custom transformation functions.

We use `earthquakes.geojson` from the **USGS real-time earthquake feed**, a live GeoJSON feed maintained by the U.S. Geological Survey providing seismic activity data including magnitude, location, depth, and timestamps.

Here is the tibble we will build:

```{r earthquakes-data}
earthquake_df
```

### Exercise 1

Download the GeoJSON file to your `data` directory. In the R Terminal, run:

```
download.file(
  "https://github.com/PPBDS/vscode.tutorials/raw/refs/heads/main/inst/tutorials/r4ds-5/data/earthquakes.geojson",
  destfile = "data/earthquakes.geojson",
  mode = "wb"
)
```

CP/CR.

```{r earthquakes-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 5)
```

###

GeoJSON is a JSON-based format for representing geographic features. A `FeatureCollection` object contains a `features` array — each feature has `type`, `geometry` (with coordinates), and `properties` (metadata like magnitude and place).

### Exercise 2

In a new code chunk in your `analysis.qmd`, paste the following code to explore the raw JSON structure:

```
jsonlite::read_json("data/earthquakes.geojson") |>
  pluck("features") |>
  tibble(data = _) |>
  unnest_wider(data) |>
  unnest_wider(properties, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(type, id, properties_mag, properties_place, properties_time) |>
  head(5)
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r earthquakes-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

```{r earthquakes-2-test}
jsonlite::read_json("data/earthquakes.geojson") |>
  pluck("features") |>
  tibble(data = _) |>
  unnest_wider(data) |>
  unnest_wider(properties, names_sep = "_") |>
  select(type, id, properties_mag, properties_place, properties_time) |>
  head(5)
```

###

`pluck()` extracts a named element from a nested list by name or index. `unnest_wider()` expands a list-column so each named element becomes its own column — use `names_sep` to avoid name collisions when unnesting multiple levels.

### Exercise 3

In your `analysis.qmd`, replace the code from the previous exercise with a new code chunk. First, ask AI to define two helper functions:

- `convert_earthquake_time(milliseconds)`: converts milliseconds since Unix epoch to a POSIXct datetime using `as.POSIXct(milliseconds / 1000, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC")`
- `categorize_magnitude(mag)`: returns one of six categories — "Minor" (< 3.0), "Light" (3.0–3.9), "Moderate" (4.0–4.9), "Strong" (5.0–5.9), "Major" (6.0–6.9), "Great" (7.0+) — using `case_when()`

Then, in a second new code chunk, ask AI to build a pipe starting with `jsonlite::read_json("data/earthquakes.geojson")` that: plucks the "features" element, converts to a tibble, unnests `data` and then `properties` (with `names_sep = "_"`), hoists `geometry` to extract `coordinates`, unnests coordinates and renames to `longitude`/`latitude`/`depth`, converts "null" strings to `NA` across all character columns, filters rows where `properties_mag`, `longitude`, or `latitude` are `NA`, applies `convert_earthquake_time()` and `categorize_magnitude()` using `as.numeric()`, converts `properties_mag` and `depth` to numeric, filters to the last 365 days and to continental US bounds (longitude −125 to −66, latitude 20 to 50), extracts `hour` and `day_of_week` from `datetime`, categorizes `depth` as Shallow/Intermediate/Deep, selects key columns, creates a `tooltip_text` string with HTML formatting, filters valid geographic bounds, adds a `color` column by magnitude, and arranges by descending magnitude. Assign to `earthquake_df`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r earthquakes-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 8)
```

###

```{r earthquakes-3-test}
earthquake_df
```

###

If your result does not match ours, replace your code with the following:

<pre><code>```{r}
convert_earthquake_time <- function(milliseconds) {
  as.POSIXct(milliseconds / 1000, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC")
}
categorize_magnitude <- function(mag) {
  case_when(
    mag < 3.0 ~ "Minor",
    mag < 4.0 ~ "Light",
    mag < 5.0 ~ "Moderate",
    mag < 6.0 ~ "Strong",
    mag < 7.0 ~ "Major",
    TRUE ~ "Great"
  )
}
```</code></pre>

<pre><code>```{r}
#| cache: true
earthquake_df <- jsonlite::read_json("data/earthquakes.geojson") |>
  pluck("features") |>
  tibble(data = _) |>
  unnest_wider(data) |>
  unnest_wider(properties, names_sep = "_") |>
  hoist(geometry, coordinates = "coordinates") |>
  unnest_wider(coordinates, names_sep = "_") |>
  rename(
    longitude = coordinates_1,
    latitude  = coordinates_2,
    depth     = coordinates_3
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~ifelse(.x == "null", NA, .x))) |>
  filter(!if_any(c(properties_mag, longitude, latitude), is.na)) |>
  mutate(
    datetime = convert_earthquake_time(as.numeric(properties_time)),
    mag_category = categorize_magnitude(as.numeric(properties_mag))
  ) |>
  mutate(across(c(properties_mag, depth), as.numeric)) |>
  filter(datetime >= Sys.Date() - 365) |>
  filter(longitude >= -125, longitude <= -66, latitude >= 20, latitude <= 50) |>
  mutate(
    hour = hour(datetime),
    day_of_week = wday(datetime, label = TRUE),
    depth_category = case_when(
      depth < 70 ~ "Shallow",
      depth < 300 ~ "Intermediate",
      depth < 600 ~ "Deep"
    )
  ) |>
  select(longitude, latitude, properties_mag, properties_place,
         datetime, depth, mag_category, depth_category) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "Magnitude: ", properties_mag, " (", mag_category, ")<br>",
      "Location: ", properties_place, "<br>",
      "Time: ", format(datetime, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"), "<br>",
      "Depth: ", round(depth, 1), " km (", depth_category, ")"
    )
  ) |>
  filter(
    longitude >= -180, longitude <= 180,
    latitude >= -90, latitude <= 90,
    properties_mag >= 0, properties_mag <= 10
  ) |>
  mutate(
    color = case_when(
      properties_mag >= 6 ~ "red",
      properties_mag >= 5 ~ "orange",
      properties_mag >= 4 ~ "yellow",
      properties_mag >= 3 ~ "green",
      TRUE ~ "blue"
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(properties_mag))
```</code></pre>

`hoist()` selectively extracts specific named values from a list-column without fully unnesting it — useful when you only need certain fields from a complex nested structure like the `geometry` object here.

### Exercise 4

Within the `earthquake_df` code chunk, add `#| cache: true` at the top. Add `analysis_cache` to your `.gitignore` if you have not already done so.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time — Quarto is running the code and saving the result to disk. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r earthquakes-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 8)
```

###

### Exercise 5

In a new code chunk, ask AI to create an interactive map of `earthquake_df` using **leaflet**. The map should display circle markers sized by magnitude and colored by the `color` column, with popups showing the `tooltip_text`. Add a legend. Do not assign the plot to a variable.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r earthquakes-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r earthquakes-5-test}
leaflet(earthquake_df) |>
  addTiles() |>
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng = ~longitude,
    lat = ~latitude,
    radius = ~properties_mag * 2,
    color = ~color,
    popup = ~tooltip_text,
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 1
  ) |>
  addLegend(
    position = "bottomright",
    colors = c("red", "orange", "yellow", "green", "blue"),
    labels = c("Major (6.0+)", "Strong (5.0-5.9)", "Moderate (4.0-4.9)",
              "Light (3.0-3.9)", "Minor (<3.0)"),
    title = "Earthquake Magnitude"
  )
```

###

`leaflet()` creates interactive HTML maps. `addTiles()` loads the background map tiles; `addCircleMarkers()` places circles at each lat/lng coordinate. The `popup` argument accepts HTML strings, which is why `tooltip_text` uses `<br>` for line breaks.

## Top Movies
###

This section covers web scraping techniques and data extraction from HTML tables using **[rvest](https://rvest.tidyverse.org/)**, **[httr2](https://httr2.r-lib.org/)**, and **[stringr](https://stringr.tidyverse.org/)**. You will scrape the archived IMDb Top 250 movies list, clean the resulting text, and extract structured data. Key concepts include `html_element()`, `html_elements()`, `html_table()`, `html_attr()`, and `separate_wider_regex()`.

We use a archived version of **The IMDb Top 250 Movies list** from February 2022, accessed via the Wayback Machine for a stable, reproducible URL.

Here is the tibble we will build:

```{r top-movies-data}
top_movies
```

### Exercise 1

In a new code chunk in your `analysis.qmd`, paste the following code to fetch and display the raw IMDb table:

```
request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
  req_perform() |>
  resp_body_html() |>
  html_element("table") |>
  html_table()
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r top-movies-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

A `robots.txt` file tells automated bots which pages the site owner permits them to access. Always check `https://example.com/robots.txt` before scraping a new site. The Wayback Machine archives are generally permitted for research scraping.

### Exercise 2

In a new code chunk in your `analysis.qmd`, paste the following code to extract the rating details — the number of user votes — stored as an HTML attribute:

```
request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
  req_perform() |>
  resp_body_html() |>
  html_elements("td strong") |>
  html_attr("title") |>
  head(5)
```

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r top-movies-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 6)
```

###

`html_elements()` selects all nodes matching a CSS selector and returns an `xml_nodeset`. `html_attr()` then extracts a named attribute from each node — here the `title` attribute on `<strong>` elements inside table cells contains the vote count string.

### Exercise 3

In your `analysis.qmd`, replace the code from the previous two exercises with a single new code chunk. Ask AI to build a pipe starting with `request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/")` that: performs the request, reads the HTML body, extracts the main `"table"` with `html_element()`, calls `html_table()`, selects only `rank_title_year = "Rank & Title"` and `rating = "IMDb Rating"`, uses `mutate()` to clean `rank_title_year` with `str_replace_all()` (replacing `"\n +"` with `" "`) and to create a `rating_details` column by embedding a second `request()` call that fetches the same URL and extracts `html_elements("td strong") |> html_attr("title")`, splits `rank_title_year` into `rank`/`title`/`year` with `separate_wider_regex()`, splits `rating_details` into a `number` column with another `separate_wider_regex()`, uses `mutate()` to apply `parse_number()` to `number`, convert `rank` and `year` to numeric with `across()`, create a `decade` column, and create `rating_category` and `popularity` columns using `case_when()`, and finally selects only `rank`, `title`, `year`, `rating`, `number` and arranges by `rank`. Assign to `top_movies`.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r top-movies-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 8)
```

###

```{r top-movies-3-test}
top_movies
```

###

If your result does not match ours, replace your code with the following:

<pre><code>```{r}
#| cache: true
top_movies <- request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
  req_perform() |>
  resp_body_html() |>
  html_element("table") |>
  html_table() |>
  select(
    rank_title_year = `Rank & Title`,
    rating = `IMDb Rating`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    rank_title_year = str_replace_all(rank_title_year, "\n +", " "),
    rating_details = request("https://web.archive.org/web/20220201012049/https://www.imdb.com/chart/top/") |>
      req_perform() |>
      resp_body_html() |>
      html_elements("td strong") |>
      html_attr("title")
  ) |>
  separate_wider_regex(
    rank_title_year,
    patterns = c(
      rank = "\\d+", "\\. ",
      title = ".+", " +\\(",
      year = "\\d+", "\\)"
    )
  ) |>
  separate_wider_regex(
    rating_details,
    patterns = c(
      "[0-9.]+ based on ",
      number = "[0-9,]+",
      " user ratings"
    )
  ) |>
  mutate(
    number = parse_number(number),
    across(c(rank, year), as.numeric),
    decade = paste0(floor(year/10)*10, "s"),
    rating_category = case_when(
      rating >= 9.0 ~ "Masterpiece (9.0+)",
      rating >= 8.5 ~ "Excellent (8.5-8.9)",
      rating >= 8.0 ~ "Great (8.0-8.4)",
      TRUE ~ "Good (<8.0)"
    ),
    popularity = case_when(
      number >= 2000000 ~ "Very Popular (2M+)",
      number >= 1000000 ~ "Popular (1M-2M)",
      number >= 500000 ~ "Moderate (500K-1M)",
      TRUE ~ "Niche (<500K)"
    )
  ) |>
  select(rank, title, year, rating, number) |>
  arrange(rank)
```</code></pre>

`separate_wider_regex()` splits a character column into multiple new columns using named capture groups in a regex pattern. Unnamed pattern pieces are consumed but discarded — only the named groups (`rank = "\\d+"`) become columns.

### Exercise 4

Within the `top_movies` code chunk, add `#| cache: true` at the top.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. This first render will take some time. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r top-movies-4}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 8)
```

###

### Exercise 5

In a new code chunk, ask AI to create an interesting plot of `top_movies`. Include a proper title, subtitle, and caption in `labs()`. Do not assign the plot to a variable.

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd`. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd", chunk = "Last")
```

CP/CR.

```{r top-movies-5}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 12)
```

###

```{r top-movies-5-test}
top_movies |>
  filter(year >= 1990, year <= 2010) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = rating)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = number / 1000), alpha = 0.8, stroke = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "navy",
              linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = ifelse(rating >= 9.0, str_wrap(title, 15), "")),
                  size = 3, max.overlaps = 8,
                  box.padding = 0.5, point.padding = 0.3) +
  scale_size_continuous(name = "Votes\n(thousands)", range = c(2, 12),
                        breaks = c(500, 1000, 1500, 2000, 2500),
                        labels = c("500", "1,000", "1,500", "2,000", "2,500")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1990, 2010, 2)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(8.0, 9.2, 0.2), limits = c(7.9, 9.3)) +
  labs(
    title = "IMDb Top 250: The Golden Age (1990–2010)",
    subtitle = "Masterpiece films labeled · Size shows popularity",
    x = "Release Year",
    y = "IMDb Rating",
    caption = "Data: IMDb Top 250 archived February 2022"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold", color = "navy"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "gray40"),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray50"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

###

`geom_text_repel()` from **ggrepel** places text labels that automatically move apart to avoid overlapping each other and the data points. `max.overlaps` controls how many overlapping labels to allow before hiding them.

## Summary
###

This section covered key concepts from [Chapter 23: Hierarchical Data](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/hierarchical), [Chapter 24: Web Scraping](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/webscraping), [Chapter 25: Functions](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/functions), and [Chapter 26: Iterations](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/iteration) from [*R for Data Science (2e)*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/) by Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund.

You learned about working with nested data structures, web scraping techniques, and creating custom functions using core packages such as **[jsonlite](https://cran.r-project.org/package=jsonlite)**, **[rvest](https://rvest.tidyverse.org/)**, **[httr2](https://httr2.r-lib.org/)**, and **[purrr](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/)**. Key concepts included extracting nested elements from lists, expanding list-columns, extracting HTML tables, and parsing complex text into structured columns using regex.

### Exercise 1

In a bash terminal, run `quarto render analysis.qmd` to ensure that everything works. Check your Live Server tab.

In the R Terminal, run:

```
show_file("analysis.qmd")
```

CP/CR.

```{r summary-1}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 30)
```

###

`purrr::map()` applies a function to each element of a list and returns the results as a new list. `map_dfr()` combines the results into a single data frame by row-binding — useful when you want to apply a scraping function to multiple URLs.

### Exercise 2

Publish your rendered QMD to GitHub Pages. In a bash terminal, run:

```
quarto publish gh-pages analysis.qmd
```

If the bash terminal is still running from rendering, stop it first using `Ctrl/Cmd + C`.

Copy/paste the resulting URL below.

```{r summary-2}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 1)
```

###

**ggplot2** can save a plot to a file with `ggsave()`. By default it saves the last displayed plot, using the current device size, and infers the file format from the extension (PNG, PDF, etc.).

### Exercise 3

Commit and push all your files. Copy/paste the URL to your GitHub repo.

```{r summary-3}
question_text(NULL,
	answer(NULL, correct = TRUE),
	allow_retry = TRUE,
	try_again_button = "Edit Answer",
	incorrect = NULL,
	rows = 3)
```

###

There is one special type of missing value that you may encounter: `NaN` (**n**ot **a** **n**umber). In the rare case you need to distinguish an `NA` from a `NaN`, use `is.nan(x)`.

```{r download-answers, child = system.file("child_documents/download_answers.Rmd", package = "tutorial.helpers")}
```
